mnemonics.title: Binary Addition Rules
mnemonics.content: When adding bits A and B: '0+0 gives 0, 0+1 gives 1, 1+0 gives 1, but 1+1 gives 0 with a carry to go' — remember Carry is generated only when both bits are 1 (A AND B).

mnemonics.title: Half Adder Formula
mnemonics.content: 'Sum sees XOR, Carry sees AND' — Sum = A XOR B, Carry = A AND B. The Sum is different inputs, Carry is same inputs.

mnemonics.title: Full Adder Sum & Carry
mnemonics.content: 'Sum is odd, Carry is majority' — Sum = A ⊕ B ⊕ Cin (odd number of 1s gives 1). Carry = (A·B) + (Cin·(A ⊕ B)) — carry if at least two inputs are 1.

mnemonics.title: 2's Complement Method
mnemonics.content: 'Flip all bits, then add 1' — To negate a binary number: invert every bit (0→1, 1→0) then add 1 to the least significant bit.

mnemonics.title: Overflow Detection Rule
mnemonics.content: 'C[n-1] XOR C[n]' — Overflow occurs in signed arithmetic when the carry into the sign bit (C[n-1]) differs from the carry out of the sign bit (C[n]).

mnemonics.title: BCD Addition Trick
mnemonics.content: If sum > 1001 (9), add 0110 (6) to skip invalid BCD digits and adjust the carry.

acronyms.usage: Used when adding 2 single-bit numbers; has Sum and Carry outputs only.
Adds 3 bits (A, B, Cin); used as building block for multi-bit adders.
Chain of FAs where each carry ripples to the next stage; simple but slow due to propagation delay.
Generates carry signals in parallel using Generate (G) and Propagate (P) terms; faster than RCA.
The core component that performs arithmetic microoperations (add, sub, inc, dec) and logical operations.
Represents each decimal digit (0-9) as a 4-bit binary code; used in displays and calculators.
Method for representing signed numbers; allows subtraction via addition of the additive inverse.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine dropping pebbles into two cups: when both cups have a pebble (1+1), they overflow and create a pebble in the next cup (carry), while the original cup returns to empty (sum=0). Think of it like counting on fingers where 9+1=0 carry 1.
Picture a row of dominoes — each FA's carry output 'tips' the next FA's carry input. The carry ripples through slowly, like a wave. This is why RCA is slow.
Imagine each FA has a telescope — it can 'look ahead' to see if any earlier column will generate a carry, without waiting for the ripple. All carries are computed simultaneously in parallel.
Visualize a light switch board — flip all switches (invert bits), then push the rightmost switch (+1). The resulting pattern represents the negative value.
Think of a cup overflowing: if you add 1 to 127 (01111111), it becomes -128 (10000000). The carry into the sign bit differs from carry out — that's your overflow alarm.

analogies.analogy: Like combining two piles of identical coins: 0+0 means empty piles stay empty; 0+1 or 1+0 means one coin in one pile transfers to result; 1+1 means two coins combine to form a new pile (carry) and leave 0 in current position.

Think of a circular odometer in a car: to subtract, you add the 'negative distance.' If odometer reads 0005 and you want to subtract 2, you add 9998 (2's complement of 2), resulting in 0003 after wrap-around.
Like pouring water between cups of fixed size: if you keep adding 250ml cups to a 1-liter container, once it exceeds 1 liter, it wraps around to show overflow — the sign bit flips unexpectedly.
Sum = 1 when an odd number of inputs are 1 (minority loses). Carry = 1 when at least 2 out of 3 vote 'yes' — majority wins.
Just as a calculator has buttons for +, −, ×, ÷, the ALU has selection lines that choose which arithmetic microoperation to perform on data inputs — it's the calculator's arithmetic engine.
